data.title: McDonald's: $5 Meal Deals Not Enough To Convince Discerning Customers
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

McDonald’s (NYSE:MCD) reported its Q2 FY24 earnings in July, where it saw its global comparable sales declining 1.0% YoY, missing analyst estimates. The underperformance was driven by negative comparable guest counts in the US and International markets, while its International Developmental Licenses Markets were impacted by the war in the Middle East and weakness in China. At the same time, consolidated operating income also decreased 6% YoY, with diluting earnings per share decreasing 11% YoY to $2.80, which also missed analyst estimates as a result of lower sales performance.

While the management attributed the weakness to the highly discerning customer, particularly among lower-income households whose budgets have been stretched from an elongated period of high inflation and interest rates, they emphasized that they are taking meaningful actions to assert their value perception and core menu innovation to gain back market share.

What is interesting to note is that despite a grim Q2 earnings report, the stock has been steadily climbing since then and has outperformed the S&P 500 during the last 3 months.

In this post, we will discuss some of those initiatives that McDonald’s has taken to revive comparable sales growth in its store with its $5 Meal Deal, attracting lower-income households, while simultaneously innovating its core menu to capture market share from competitors in categories such as chicken and beef and grow its loyalty membership.

However, I believe that its rally will soon run out of gas as its stock is priced to perfection, given future growth estimates, where both revenue and earnings are expected to recover into FY25. At the same time, McDonald’s is also trading at a premium relative to many of its competitors, which I believe is not justified given the projected growth rates. Finally, macroeconomic headwinds continue to persist as the monetary easing cycle is uncertain, which can dampen consumer sentiment, profitability, and sales performance in the coming quarters.

Therefore, assessing both the “good” and the “bad”, I have rated the stock a “hold”, with a price target of $284.

The good: $5 Meal Deal, Menu Innovations & Loyalty Membership growth

Restoring value perception with $5 Meal Deal

After losing its value perception with higher prices and declining prices, its $5 Meal Deal, which launched in late June with a 4-piece Chicken McNuggets selection, fries, a drink, and the customer's choice of either a McChicken or McDouble, is attracting consumers and is profitable for 96% of restaurants.

It is important to note that the timing of the meal deal was not fully reflected in the company’s Q2 sales, but Chris Kempczinski, CEO of McDonald's, noted that the average check has been over $10 for the $5 Meal Deal, which indicates strong add-ons as part of the program as it restores traffic to the chain.

What I believe is quite optimistic is that same store sales trends have turned positive since the back half of August with growing visits as highlighted by analysts at Evercore ISI and BTIG. This could translate into positive comparable sales growth for Q3 and Q4 as the company’s service metrics improve alongside marketing and value.

At the same time, McDonald’s announced last month that it will extend its $5 Meal Deal at the majority of its restaurants through the end of December, with McDonald’s US President Joe Erlinger reiterating their commitment to keeping prices as affordable as possible. At the same time, McDonald’s is also offering a $2 McCrispy sandwich in honor of National Fried Chicken Sandwich Day on November 9 and will continue to celebrate Free Fries Fridays along with local deals as they innovate on ways to offer meals at fair prices.

Innovating its core menu with the Big Arch, Chicken Big Mac & Expanding Breakfast Options

Meanwhile, McDonald’s continues to innovate across their core menu to address unmet customer needs with the introduction of The Big Arch, which they piloted across three international markets to satisfy larger appetites with its enhanced flavors and sizes. The Big Arch features two 100% Canadian beef quarter-pound patties and three slices of white processed cheese, which are topped with crispy onions, slivered onions, pickles, lettuce, and a new tangy sauce served on a sesame and poppy seed bun.

During the Q2 earnings call, Chris Kempczinski called the Big Arch the chain’s "quintessential" new item, with a twist on the chain's iconic familiar flavors. So far, reviews of the Big Arch sandwich on social media sites Instagram, TikTok, Facebook, and X have been generally positive.

Looking ahead, McDonald’s is planning a large rollout of The Big Arch in the US, which could further boost comparable sales in its US markets while adding incremental competition for other burger chains such as Wendy’s (NASDAQ:WEN), Burger King (NYSE:QSR), Jack in the Box (NASDAQ:JACK), Shake Shack (NYSE:SHAK), and more.

Simultaneously, the management has repeatedly noted that they see a significant growth opportunity in chicken, which is a category that is twice the size of beef globally and growing at a faster rate. In early October, the company launched the Chicken Big Mac for a limited time, which is priced at $5.49 in most markets to attract both loyal fans and newcomers.

Although the reviews have been mixed, McDonald’s saw visits jump by 7.9% compared to the chain’s YTD Thursday visit average, which showcases the power of limited-time items to generate excitement and urgency among consumers.

Finally, in addition to new menu items, McDonald’s is placing a strong emphasis on its breakfast offerings as it expands its breakfast menu, offering healthier alternatives and promoting limited time deals. This has successfully translated into higher morning traffic, with 24.8% of daily visits occurring between 5:00 am and 11:00 am.

Steadily growing loyalty membership will lead to higher order values and purchase frequency

As McDonald’s works to restore its value perception with its $5 Meal Plan, while innovating on its core menu to gain market share from its competitors in categories such as chicken and beef, its third growth lever is tied to its loyalty membership, which surpassed expectations at 166M members, representing 25% of system-wide sales. The company has a target of reaching 250M members as they aim to scale their number of restaurant locations to 50,000 by 2027 with a digital-forward, values-driven, and culture-led strategy.

The reason why growing loyalty members is a priority is because loyalty customers tend to spend more and visit more often. Therefore, as McDonald’s invests in unlocking deeper customer insights, they will be able to drive relevant, timely, and more personalized offers, which should translate to a higher frequency of orders and size of order value per customer, along with elevated customer satisfaction levels.

The bad: Valuation Concerns, Competitive Pressures and Persisting Macroeconomic Headwinds

McDonald’s stock seems fully priced.

If I take the consensus estimates for its revenue and earnings projections for the next 3 years into account, where revenue is estimated to grow roughly 2.5% YoY in FY24 before turning to growth in the mid single digits after that, it should generate close to $28.8B by FY26. I believe this will be achievable as McDonald’s restores its value perception to attract lower-income households with programs like the $5 Meal Plan, while simultaneously innovating on its core menu to convert its new and existing users into loyalty members, where it can leverage user insights to deepen engagement and purchase size and frequency.

Meanwhile, from a profitability standpoint, taking the consensus estimates for non-GAAP EPS of $13.80 in FY26, we see that it will be growing at a slightly faster rate than overall revenue growth. This will be equivalent to a present value of $11.83 in EPS when discounted at 8%.

Taking the S&P 500 as a proxy, where its companies their earnings increase on average by 8% over a 10-year period, with a price-to-earnings ratio of 15-18 and a current forward price-to-earnings ratio of 22, I believe McDonald’s should trade at par with the index, which will translate to a price target of $260, which represents a downside of 10% from its current levels.

Therefore, even though McDonald’s revenue and earnings are projected to improve from next year onwards, I believe its stock price has fully priced in the upside and is not trading at an attractive level from a risk-reward perspective.

Its competitors are growing faster and are priced more attractively.

Meanwhile, McDonald’s stock also carries a higher premium relative to its competitors, which include Wendy’s, Burger King, Jack in the Box, Shake Shack, Yum Brands, and more. With the exception of Wendy’s and Jack in the Box, the remaining competitors are growing their revenues at a faster rate than McDonald’s over the next few years and are trading at a more attractive twelve-month forward price-to-earnings ratio. Plus, with the exception of Shake Shack, McDonald’s and its competitors have been underperforming the index year-to-date.

Macroeconomic uncertainty can threaten sales performance in domestic and international markets.

While McDonald’s is working hard to restore its value perception as it tries to gain market share among lower income households while innovating on its core menu to build loyalty, the US consumer continues to be highly discerning as an elevated period of high inflation and interest rates has squeezed consumer budgets. Although the Fed recently cut rates by 50 basis points in its last meeting, inflation can spike once again, which will derail the Fed’s monetary easing cycle plans. This will not only hurt consumer sentiment but also affect the profitability of McDonald’s with increased cost of goods as well as wage inflation. At the same time, a higher dollar could also threaten performance in its international markets, which will hurt overall sales performance.

My final verdict and conclusions

Assessing both the “good” and the "bad," I believe that the recent rally in McDonald’s stock price will likely run out of legs soon. While its initiatives to restore its value perception with the $5 Meal Value, along with innovations to its core menu with The Big Arch, Chicken Big Mac and more, should result in positive comparable sales growth in Q3 and possibly into Q4, I believe that the stock has fully priced in the upside with limited risk-reward to initiate a new position at its current levels. Furthermore, McDonald’s stock is also trading at a steeper premium despite slower revenue growth rates when compared to its competitors, along with persisting macroeconomic headwinds that can derail progress in its domestic and international markets. Therefore, I will rate the stock a “hold” at its current levels with a price target of $284.

data.title: Silver: 12-Year Highs But Buyer Beware
data.description:
I’m not a silver or gold bug, but I have been fairly bullish on silver in the past year or so. Shown below are my previous analysis on (NYSEARCA:SLV) (ETF tracks physical silver).

This article is an update as physical spot silver prices soared over $33/oz today, the highest in 12 years since it peaked at near $50/ounce in 2011.

There has been a lot of buzz on social media given the appreciation in silver prices. While I continue to see the long-term potential in silver, in the short-run I think a few observations suggest caution is warranted, especially for new or small individual investors that get swept up by the hype of rapidly appreciating prices.

Caution Point #1: Commercials in the COT (Commitments of Traders) report are still short

There is a lot of discussion/books that mention the COT as a useful indicator, in short, it is typically believed to be bullish when the Commercial traders (e.g. non-financial companies that actually produce and use the metal) have increased their net long (or reduced net short) position and vice versa.

If we take a look at silver’s COT positioning, in the below chart, the red line below signifies the Commercial Traders and it has been quite prescient:

  • Note how the red line went up (reflecting a reduction in net shorts) in Mar/Apr-20 when silver prices crashed during the COVID panic. Silver prices soon recovered.
  • Commercials positioning went sideways for the next two years, and silver prices languished as well.
  • Note how in Q4-2022, commercial positioning in silver was the highest net long in the entire 5-year period. While silver prices still bounced around a bit, eventually they started an upward trend.

With the strong performance of silver in 2024, commercial positioning has reached the most negative levels since before COVID panic. I wouldn’t necessarily view this as “bearish” as I would not use this indicator as a “silver bullet”, however the COT commercial positioning is definitely not issuing a strong buy signal.

Caution Point #2: Inventories

Silver COMEX inventories increased from 2016 to 2021 (which probably kept silver prices low during this period). Inventories started declining in 2021 from 400 million ounces to 270 million ounces by year-end 2023.

However, COMEX inventories have been steadily increasing this year to 306 million ounces at Oct. 16, 2024.

Likewise for the LBMA vault of silver holdings, which declined from 1.2 billion ounces in 2021 to 800 million ounces in 2023. Yet, there have been no further drawdowns in 2024, but rather a small increase instead.

Both of the above suggest to me that the supply/demand of silver this year is likely fairly balanced, and the recent surge in price is mainly driven by factors beyond supply/demand.

Caution Point #3: Rapidly appreciating silver prices

If we look closely at the chart of historical silver prices above, it’s quite evident that silver prices are typically flat for many years and go up in short upward bursts that are typically followed by long periods of stagnation. Unlike an individual stock like Coca-Cola or Google that can become hundred or thousand baggers and continue their upward trajectory indefinitely, silver’s fate is probably a lot more mundane.

While there are some “true believer” type silver bugs that compare silver and gold ratios and extrapolate silver prices to mind-boggling levels, I would have more realistic expectations: Silver is a nice commodity to have that has endured throughout the ages and probably will outlast many other assets (that get disrupted by economic and macro changes) and purchased at a low enough price is probably worth it as a storage of value. But it’s probably not a good idea to purchase at any price, especially after an almost 40% run this year, as the higher the purchase price the lower the margin of safety and the higher the risk of ending up holding silver through a lengthy unprofitable period.

Caution Point #4: General hype around silver

When gold and silver rise in prices, it’s typically surrounded by a lot of hype about USD weakness. While no doubt there is some fundamental truth in the hype, this is a process with a very long time horizon. In fact, peak hype may coincide with peak prices.

For example, in 2011, S&P’s decision to downgrade the USA’s credit rating was much hyped and coincided with the peak in gold and silver prices. It turns out that this isolated event probably had limited impact on USD, gold and silver values, even over a 5 or 10-year period. Since that pivotal event, gold and silver prices languished for a decade as higher gold and silver prices induced more production and gold/silver fell out of focus as the market shifted to other profitable investments (e.g. stocks).

Today, the rapidly growing US national debt is the focus of much attention. While this phenomenon is indeed bearish for the USD in the long run, it probably has limited impact on silver prices in the short run. In other words, just because the US is running a $1.8 trillion deficit annually, does not translate into immediate vast appreciation of silver prices. The $1.8 trillion deficit adds about 5% to the US national debt annually and while this continuously chips away at the USD’s value, the impact on silver is probably more circuitous and indirect.

Risks to caution thesis

That said, silver is in a bull market now, and I would definitely NOT go short. The market could continue bidding up silver prices as the massive peace-time deficits by the USA erode the USD’s value and, as in 2011, silver prices could surge to eye-watering (and perhaps even irrational) levels.

Conclusion

I would suggest investors treat the latest high in silver prices with caution as COT Commercial traders data is not bullish and inventories data actually show increased inventories. Those of us that have been around for a few decades remember the last several times social media was blaring with doom and gloom and how that more often than not coincided with a peak in gold/silver prices.

For investors with a long time horizon, it’s fine to keep holding silver (or maybe taking some gains), but I would definitely caution new investors against rushing into silver at these prices.

data.title: Nvidia: After Understanding This, My Only Regret Is That I Didn't Buy More
data.description:
Most analysts covering NVIDIA (NASDAQ:NVDA) (NEOE:NVDA:CA) are currently focused on GPU demand from data centers as signals for future stock price performance.

In fact, in the previous article, NVDA stock was downgraded to a ‘hold’ as signs of Nvidia losing pricing power and profit margin compression raise the risk of a massive pullback in the share price. We also covered how the rise of open-source software threatens Nvidia’s competitive moat, as per historical analogies from previous tech cycles.

Though Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang did come out recently to proclaim that “demand for Blackwell is insane”, sending the stock rallying to reach new all-time highs, and admittedly subduing the chances of a major pullback any time soon.

Although, selling AI chips to data center customers to power increasingly versatile Large Language Models (LLMs) is only a fraction of the growth story.

The truth is, Nvidia is also strongly positioned to capitalize on the next leg of this AI revolution, and that is the robotics phase. Beyond the hardware opportunities, it is the beautifully recurring software revenue potential that makes one wish they had bought more shares.

The robotics revolution

By now, most people will have seen CEO Jensen Huang show off a fleet of robots at various Nvidia events, all of various different sizes, shapes and dexterities, fit for a variety of different jobs.

Over time, robots are expected to fill jobs in numerous sectors and industries, including retail, hospitality, and healthcare. Although for now, industries like warehousing and manufacturing remain the most prominent fields of deployment.

Base case is for more than 250,000 humanoid robot shipments in 2030, almost all of which would be for industrial use.

– Goldman Sachs

At the COMPUTEX event back in June 2024, CEO Jensen Huang highlighted the essential hardware solutions that Nvidia offers to help companies build and operate their robotic devices:

Building robots with generative physical AI requires three computers: NVIDIA AI supercomputers to train the models; NVIDIA Jetson Orin and next generation Jetson Thor Robotics supercomputer to run the models; and NVIDIA Omniverse, where robots can learn and refine their skills in simulated worlds. We build the platforms, acceleration libraries and AI models needed by developers and companies and allow them to use any or all of the stacks that suit them best.

One very advantageous trend working in Nvidia’s favor is the growing use of demonstrative videos to train robots, as opposed to developers having to code for every single task and circumstance that the robot may face.

We have this technology called Teleoperation. The person does the thing 200 times, we record all that data, and then we use that data to train these models. And the AI models are very similar to the GPT style generative AI models. You feed in the 200 trajectories and the system learns how the task is being done, and then the robot will do the task autonomously.

– Geordie Rose, Co-founder and CEO of Sanctuary AI in an interview with CNBC

The reason this is such a favorable trend is because video-based training requires a lot more computing power, conducive to greater demand for more proficient chips and powerful NVIDIA AI supercomputers.

Automation software layer

The sale of increasingly powerful hardware computing solutions is just one component of the growth opportunity, as the greater value resides in recurring software revenue. This is where Nvidia’s Omniverse comes in.

One of the integral technologies for advancing robotics is reinforcement learning. Just as LLMs need RLHF or reinforcement learning from human feedback. To learn particular skills, generative physical AI can learn skills using reinforcement learning from physics feedback in a simulated world. These simulation environments are where robots learn to make decisions by performing actions in a virtual world that obeys the laws of physics.

We built NVIDIA Omniverse as the operating system where physical AIs can be created. Omniverse is a development platform for virtual world simulation, combining real time, physically based rendering from physics simulation and generative AI technologies.

- Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang, COMPUTEX 2024 (emphasis added)

Now, this concept of the NVIDIA Omniverse as an operating system is key to the recurring software revenue prospects through automation and robotics. In a previous article, we had extensively delved into Nvidia’s other ‘operating system’ offering, known as ‘NVIDIA AI Enterprise’, in which we explained the software revenue opportunities using current examples from the ongoing AI revolution in the LLM space.

NVIDIA AI Enterprise is $4,500 per GPU per year, that's our business model. Our business model is basically a license. Our customers then with that basic license can build their monetization model on top of.

In a lot of ways we're wholesale, they become retail. They could have a per -- they could have subscription license base, they could per instance or they could do per usage, there is a lot of different ways that they could take a -- create their own business model, but ours is basically like a software license, like an operating system.

- Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang, Q3 2024 Nvidia Earnings Call

More specifically, Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) companies like ServiceNow (NOW) are already paying $4,500 per GPU per year to access NVIDIA AI Enterprise, on which they run their generative AI-powered, subscription-based, enterprise software applications in this new era.

Now as the robotics revolution gets going, Nvidia is also setting itself up to earn a similar stream of recurring software revenue through ‘NVIDIA Omniverse Enterprise,’ for which it also charges $4,500 per GPU/per year.

In fact, key Nvidia customer Agility Robotics, which is the maker of the ‘Digit’ humanoid robots, is already offering insights into what a “Robots-as-a-Service (RaaS)” world could look like going forward.

We envision an app store for robots out in the future where if for example you need a recycling app, you can go into the App Store and download that onto your robot.

As long as they have the robot, we help maintain it, take care of it, keep the software updated and they just pay on a monthly basis. - Peggy Johnson, CEO of Agility Robotics in an interview with CNBC

Akin to how software developers are able to build and monetize apps that run on PCs and smartphones, third-party developers will also be able to extend the capabilities of a robot by building software applications that enable various new functionalities beyond what the initial robot manufacturer may have envisioned.

So just like how SaaS vendors like ServiceNow are earning subscription revenue from enterprises at the front-end, while paying $4,500 per GPU/per year for the ‘NVIDIA AI Enterprise’ operating system at the back-end, RaaS vendors of the future are likely to charge monthly subscription fees to customers at the front-end, while paying a licensing fee of $4,500 per GPU/per year for the ‘NVIDIA Omniverse Enterprise’ operating system at the back-end.

Therefore, the software revenue scope through the robotics form factor could be massive. Late last year, Nvidia projected that the long-term annual market opportunity for both its operating systems, ‘NVIDIA AI Enterprise’ and ‘NVIDIA Omniverse Enterprise,’ to be $150 billion each.

Essentially, at least 30% of Nvidia’s total revenue is expected to derive from software licensing fees over the long term.

Risks and counterarguments

Safety & Regulations: One of the biggest obstacles to deployment of robots in the industrial space is safety, while increased government regulations and scrutiny is expected to protect workers from potential accidents due to robots malfunctioning.

Now, the ‘glass half empty’ viewpoint here would be that the drafting of legislature and the time lag until the rollout of updated safety regulations for industrial workplaces could slow the adoption of robotics. The ultimate stringency of such new laws could also potentially undermine the rate at which corporations adopt robots in the workplace.

That being said, the ‘glass half full’ perspective would argue that building robots, both at the hardware and software layers, that comply with constantly evolving regulations, should further boost demand for Nvidia’s omniverse technology to be able to test robots in safe environments before they are deployed in real work environments.

In Omniverse, robots can learn how to be robots. They learn how to autonomously manipulate objects with precision, such as grasping and handling objects, or navigate environments autonomously, finding optimal paths while avoiding obstacles and hazards. Learning in Omniverse minimizes the sim-to-real gap and maximizes the transfer of learned behavior.

- Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang, COMPUTEX 2024

For the most part, up till now, industrial robots have been designed to “operate away from human workers for safety reasons”, but this could be changing.

So right now Digit is non-collaborative and we're working to what's called collaborative safety, which is being able to formally verify, according to international standards, that Digit is safe to be in close proximity to a person.

- Pras Velagapudi, CTO of Agility Robotics in an interview with CNBC

This trend of machines and humans working alongside each other in the industrial sector can be favorable for Nvidia, as it would support the need for more human-like interaction capabilities being built into these robots so that they can follow instructions from their fellow human colleagues. Comprehension of natural language instructions is more compute-intensive than pushing buttons to get a robot to complete various tasks, amplifying the need for more powerful robotics chips and software extensions from Nvidia.

Cost of adoption: At Tesla’s ‘We, Robot’ event last week, CEO Elon Musk suggested that its ‘Optimus’ robot would cost between $20,000 and $30,000. In the past, he had suggested that humanoid robots would need to cost less than $20,000 to induce wider-scale adoption. Any additional software applications with monthly subscription fees would further add to the total cost of owning/ running these robots.

Although it is worth noting that the cost of manufacturing is certainly improving, augmenting the prospects of mass-scale deployment.

There are signs that robot components, from high-precision gears to actuators, could also cost less than previously expected, leading to faster commercialization. The manufacturing cost of humanoid robots has dropped — from a range that ran between an estimated $50,000 (for lower-end models) and $250,000 (for state-of-the art versions) per unit last year, to a range of between $30,000 and $150,000 now. Where our analysts had expected a decline of 15-20% per annum, the cost declined 40%.

– Goldman Sachs

The improving affordability of humanoid robots certainly benefits Nvidia in the form of higher demand for supercomputing chips, as well as growing streams of recurring software revenue through ‘NVIDIA Omniverse Enterprise.’

However, there are more impediments arising that may considerably slow down the adoption of industrial robots.

The rapidly developing automation technology can help speed up operations and lift some of the burden off human workers, but the tools have a new set of requirements such as access to far more electrical power and a strong internet signal.

Building that capability can be particularly difficult for industrial operators that are located in rural areas far from existing infrastructure, or those in urban areas where there are heavy demands on the power grid. – The Wall Street Journal

Upgrading public infrastructure to accommodate for a new industrial revolution could take years, delaying robotic deployments and abating the pace of revenue growth for Nvidia’s ‘Professional Visualization’ segment, which houses the Omniverse, and the ‘Automotive’ segment, which includes its robotics business line.

The investment case for Nvidia

On the Q2 2025 Nvidia earnings call, CFO Colette Kress offered the following remarks on the segments serving the industrial digitalization wave:

Moving to Pro visualization. Revenue of $454 million was up 6% sequentially and 20% year-on-year. Demand is being driven by AI and graphic use cases, including model fine-tuning and Omniverse-related workloads. Automotive and manufacturing were among the key industry verticals driving growth this quarter.

…

Moving to automotive and robotics, revenue was $346 million, up 5% sequentially and up 37% year-on-year. Year-on-year growth was driven by the new customer ramps in self-driving platforms

Now revenue of a few hundred million dollars dwarfs in comparison to the $26 billion in ‘Data Center’ revenue last quarter, still boasting year-on-year growth rates of 100%+.

So the ‘Professional Visualization’ and ‘Automotive’ segments are certainly not the key growth drivers for NVDA at the moment. Although that does not mean that these segments can never achieve the kind of growth rates that the ‘Data Center’ unit is currently delivering.

It ultimately comes down to the presence of a ‘killer application’. Similar to how the launch of ChatGPT spurred corporate attention to the power of generative AI and subsequently triggered the triple-digit growth rates for Nvidia’s ‘Data Center’ segment, it is simply a matter of time until a revolutionary app arises for the robotics world that would accelerate industrial adoption and stimulate faster revenue growth for Nvidia’s ‘Professional Visualization’ and ‘Automotive’ segments.

For instance, a key selling point of robots is that they can be deployed for tasks that would be considered dangerous for human laborers. Now as the industry evolves, the innovation of new robotic form factors and the simultaneous development of creative software apps should cultivate into completely new ways of working, such as in the fields of manufacturing or warehousing. Such innovations could give rise to new processes that companies could have never imagined in human-dominated work environments, or even new products and services for that matter.

As the current generative AI revolution has proven, it only takes one ‘killer application’ to spark wide-scale adoption of the underlying technology.

Likewise, it could only take one “killer application” to induce mass-scale robotics adoption and set off the next leg of Nvidia’s growth story.

Now, while the Forward PE multiple is commonly used to assess stock valuations, the Forward PEG ratio is actually a more valuable metric, as it adjusts the Forward PE multiple by the projected earnings growth rates.

The expected EPS FWD Long-Term Growth (3-5Y CAGR) for NVDA is 38.19%, which is higher than the growth rates of most large-cap tech stocks that are also anticipated to benefit from the AI revolution.

Based on these anticipated EPS growth rates, we derive the following Forward PEG ratios for each AI stock.

For context, a ratio of 1x would mean that a stock is trading close to its fair value.

At a Forward PEG multiple of 1.26x, NVDA is currently one of the cheapest AI stocks.

Keep in mind that the projected EPS growth rate for Nvidia is also predominantly based on data center revenue prospects amid the current generative AI revolution. In the event that we get a “killer application” for the robotics revolution, it would spur the market to raise their projected EPS growth rates even higher, as the growth opportunities for ‘Professional Visualization’ and ‘Automotive’ segments are augmented.

So NVDA may be even cheaper than the current valuation metrics reflect if we indeed get this ‘killer application’ in the next 3-5 years.

At present, Tesla seems to be getting most of the attention when it comes to the growth opportunities around robotics. Though with the stock trading at a lofty Forward PEG multiple of 7.72x, and its recent ‘We, Robot’ event underwhelming investors, you may be better off buying NVDA at this appealing valuation, as the Jensen Huang-led company is also aptly positioned to benefit from multiple iterations of robotic evolutions until they are perfected for official public launch.

Now given my bullish stance on NVDA, one may be confused by my ‘hold’ rating on the stock. Quite frankly, my rating is simply reflective of the fact that I would not add more to my position at these levels following such a staggering rally. Particularly as speculative behavior around the stock through leveraged ETFs like GraniteShares 2x Long NVDA Daily ETF (NVDL) becomes concerningly prevalent, as this can worsen stock volatility and the probability of a major pullback as these weak hands sell out of the stock on the slightest bad news.

That being said, for those that have been on the sidelines and eager to buy the stock right now, just stay ready to deploy a ‘dollar-cost averaging’ purchasing strategy in the event of a correction creating lower price-entry points.

For the long-term, Nvidia remains a solid stock to hold on to.

data.title: JEPI: An Opportunity With Election Risk Priced In
data.description:
Something unusual is happening in markets. Of course, it’s related to the November 5th US general election. Implied volatility looking out 30 days is elevated while near-term volatility, as measured by the 9-day VIX, is muted. The Daily Shot put out a revealing chart earlier this month, noting the difference between the traditional 30-day VIX and realized volatility.

There’s a clear gap, helping investors visualize the significant premium put on options pricing among large-cap stocks. I assert that this market quirk presents an opportunity for investors to sell those expensive options ahead of when voters head to the ballot box in early November.

One way to play it is through the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (NYSEARCA:JEPI). I am upgrading the option-selling fund that holds low-volatility stocks from a hold to a buy. Shares are up just shy of 9% (total return) since my Q2 analysis, outpacing the S&P 500 by about two percentage points.

My thesis was that momentum among the Magnificent Seven stocks would continue and result in negative alpha for JEPI. That played out for a few weeks, but the market turned in value investors’ favor starting in mid-July. Since then, the Mag 7 has taken a back seat to more diversified strategies. Let’s get a refresher on JEPI today and where it might go into year-end.

A High VIX Indicates Elevated Implied Volatility Over the Next 30 Days; Realized (Actual) Volatility Has Been Low

9-Day VIX Points To Low Volatility Through October

JEPI vs SPY: A Mid-July Relative Strength Inflection

According to the issuer, JEPI generates income through a combination of selling options and investing in U.S. large-cap stocks, seeking to deliver a monthly income stream from associated option premiums and stock dividends. The ETF's manager constructs a diversified, low-volatility equity portfolio through a proprietary research process designed to identify over- and undervalued stocks with attractive risk/return characteristics. Overall, JEPI seeks to deliver a significant portion of the returns associated with the S&P 500 Index with less volatility, in addition to monthly income.

JEPI is one of the so-called “boomer candy” ETFs, and it has grown since my June analysis. Total assets under management are now $36.8 billion, up from $33 billion four months ago. The fund features a modest 0.35% annual expense ratio, while its forward dividend yield is very high at 7.1% as of October 18, 2024. Of course, a large portion of that yield comes from selling call options.

Share-price momentum is rated a C by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system, but the fund has been impressive in terms of risk-adjusted returns in the last handful of months, and particularly so following the early August dip. JEPI sports a strong A- Risk rating given its diversified portfolio and modest historical standard deviation trends. Liquidity is another strong point for the ETF – average daily volume is almost three million shares, while its 30-day median bid/ask spread is tight at just two basis points, per J.P. Morgan Asset Management.

Digging into the portfolio, the 5-star, Bronze-rated ETF by Morningstar is primarily a large-cap fund. Just 20% of aggregate exposure is to the mid-cap space, and there is no small-cap access (per Morningstar's classification rules). JEPI boasts a 20.8x price-to-earnings ratio, which is actually more than a full handle below that of the S&P 500. The long-term EPS growth rate is healthy at 13.1%, resulting in a decent PEG ratio of 1.59x.

JEPI: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

JEPI’s sector exposure is much more diverse than the cap-weighted S&P 500. Just 20% of the ETF is invested in the Information Technology sector, compared to more than 30% of tech exposure in the SPX.

Utilities are a notable overweight at 6% today – that feature has resulted in JEPI solid absolute and relative returns since the summer. Utilities is the best-performing sector since mid-July, while I.T. is the second-worst area of the US large cap space in that time.

JEPI: Holdings & Dividend Information

S&P 500 Sector Returns Last Three Months: Utilities Leading, Tech Lagging

Seasonally, there isn’t a whole lot to go off of with JEPI since the product’s inception was in 2020. Still, October and November have been strong months, and the ETF is higher for three months running this year. Returns have tended to be soft in the first two months of the year.

JEPI: Limited Seasonal History, But Bullish Year-End Tendency

The Technical Take

With a decent PEG ratio right now (better compared to during June) and a bid to option premium today, JEPI’s chart is impressive. Of course, technical analysis on a yield-focused strategy is a bit less useful than on a traditional fund, but there are still clues to be captured. I noted support around $55 in Q2, the JEPI indeed attracted buyers there during the Tokyo Crash Monday on August 5. Another factor affecting my rating upgrade is that the ETF rose through previously noted resistance at $58.

Notice in the chart below that JEPI has rallied significantly after breaking out above the March high. Toss in dividends, and the total return is even better. Today, I expect former resistance to be new support, so keep your eye on the $58 mark. On the upside, I see a measured move bullish price objective to near $61 based on the $3 height of the previous range. The April 2022 high of $62.60 is another area to monitor.

Also, take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph – it is ranging in a bullish zone between 40 and 70 while there’s now a high amount of volume by price starting near $57 and down to $54. Finally, with a rising long-term 200-day moving average, the bulls control the primary trend.

JEPI: Rallies Through Resistance After Holding $55 Support, Rising 200dma, Strong RSI Momentum

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on JEPI. I see the covered call strategy as attractive today with elevated implied volatility levels across US large-cap stocks. With better relative strength and an ongoing broadening pattern playing out, more upside is likely through the end of 2024.

data.title: ASML Is Approaching My Buy Price - Here Is When I'll Start Buying
data.description:
Introduction

Perhaps the most important thing for investors to understand about themselves is the level of medium and long-term returns they are trying to achieve. If your returns goals are not ambitious, then far more stocks will look like good values at higher prices. In that situation, the investor mostly just needs to focus on the quality of the business rather than price and valuation because the primary goal is to prevent losses rather than achieve above-average returns. However, if an investor is seeking superior medium-term and long-term returns, the task of selecting stocks becomes more challenging (as it should). One needs to be very careful about what they are paying for the stocks they buy. I am one of those investors who aim for higher-than-average long-term returns. My baseline goal is an 8% CAGR plus inflation. (And because there will always be some losers in the mix, the threshold of expected returns for individual purchases usually needs to be even higher than that baseline to make up for the inevitable underperformers.) While during the current bull market, these levels of returns haven't been particularly hard for an index like the S&P 500 (SPY) to achieve, over the long term, it has been more difficult.

Since inception, even with the market at all-time highs, SPY has returned slightly under the 8% real return goal, with a 7.94% real annual return. The only periods since the dot.com crash SPY has exceeded this goal were the 2007 peak before the 2008 recession and the 2022 peak before inflation had worked its way into the economic data. Roughly 19 of the past 20 years, SPY hasn't achieved an 8% real CAGR when measured from the inception of the ETF in the mid-1990s.

Only the best investors, like Warren Buffett, have been able to navigate the dot.com bust and the GFC and do better than an 8% real CAGR during most time frames. Ray Dalio is another, but his Pure Alpha Fund return data are harder to come by. Most of the estimates I've seen show that Bridgewater's fund has had similar returns since the mid-1900s to those of Berkshire.

Why do I bring this up in an ASML (NASDAQ:ASML) article, you might ask? I do so because the most common critique of my strategies is that my buy prices are too low and will never occur. I partially understand this critique because if one only reads my public articles, it seems like I don't buy many stocks. But the reality is that since I started my Investing Group, The Cyclical Investor's Club, in 2019, I've purchased over 160 positions. It's just that only a small portion of those make it into articles outside the service, and since 2021, an even smaller portion than normal have done so. Another misconception seems to arise because, in a disproportionate number of my articles, I am warning investors about downside risks they might not have considered. This can make it seem like I am perpetually afraid, and hoarding cash in my mattress, waiting for the next big calamity before buying anything. But even though I've mostly been in a "risk off" mode since early 2022, in the past 12 months I've purchased 23 positions.

The truth is that I spend a lot of time studying individual stock histories because historical patterns can be a good guide to the future. Human psychology hasn't changed for thousands of years, and the economic system in the West hasn't changed much for about 500 years, except for central banking, which came along a bit over 300 years ago. When we put these facts together, historical patterns, particularly those that occurred outside of recent history, have a lot of predictive power. And history is full of situations, both for markets and individual companies, when it was precisely the point at which observers thought downside risk had been eliminated, or was out-of-mind, when these markets and companies reached their zenith and started to decline or crash. The conditions in the semiconductor industry have been ripe with this sort of thinking since AI-themed investing captured the imagination of investors a couple of years ago, ASML included.

History can help frame what is possible for businesses and stocks in the future. So in this article, I will share ASML's historical earnings and stock price patterns, and use them to help guide me to a strategy that has a high probability of producing the high medium-term returns I aim for as an investor, while also reducing the depth of potential drawdowns.

Historical Earnings Cyclicality

The first task with any stock is to analyze and categorize the historical earnings patterns. The key question I want to know is how cyclical earnings have been. This informs me which investing strategy is best suited for the stock.

I have circled the years in red in the FAST Graph above where ASML had negative earnings growth. We can see that they experienced a very deep earnings drawdown in 2008/9 that resulted in fully negative earnings. They also experienced negative earnings following the dot com crash, though it is not pictured on the graph. Most declines since the last real recession in 2009 have been shallow to moderate in nature.

When businesses have a pattern of earnings declines that exceed -50%, and then have those earnings recover in a timely manner, I classify those businesses as "Deep Cyclicals". This classification is important because when a business has deeply cyclical earnings, traditional earnings or free cash flow-based analyses are not very useful at predicting the medium-term movements of the stock price. For example, in 2007 ASML had a P/E ratio of around 23 near its peak. It had almost the same P/E ratio of 22 at the March 2009 bottom, even though the stock price had dropped about -65%. This is because earnings had also fallen about -60% at that point. A 22-23 P/E would have both been a horrible valuation to buy the stock...and a great valuation to buy the stock. In other words, earnings were not very useful to help determine a good price to pay for ASML due to its deep earnings cyclicality.

Because of this dynamic, once it has been established a business has deeply cyclical earnings, I use historical stock price patterns to help estimate good prices at which to buy the stocks as a medium-term investment.

Historical ASML Price Cyclicality

Because I cover and monitor hundreds of stocks, I perform my stock analyses in stages. First, I classify what type of business I'm dealing with, and whether there is enough historical data to be useful. If I establish it's a business I can reasonably analyze with some confidence, and it fits the parameters of one of my strategies, then, I establish a buy price where if everything checks out, I would be willing to take a position. If the stock price eventually gets close to my buy price, I do deeper research to see if there is anything that might prevent me from buying the stock. This is the opposite of how most other investors do things. Most investors (if they conduct any research at all) perform lots of research on the business before they consider buying the stock or examining the valuation. I find this is often wasted time because many of the best businesses can go years without trading at reasonable valuations that would entice me to buy them. And I need good valuations to achieve the above-average returns I'm looking for.

Above is a historical stock price drawdown chart for ASML that goes back to the mid-1990s. We see that the stock experienced a roughly -65% drawdown in 1998 before fully recovering by 2000, and then experiencing a massive -90% decline. Ultimately, it took about 12 years for ASML to recover its 2000 peak price. This is important information for investors to know because it shows what can happen after a super-cyclical peak (or bubble). Typically, I want a cyclical stock to recover its previous peak within about 5 years. Usually, I only buy deep cyclical stocks when they are at least -50% off their highs, so if the price recovers within 5 years, I get a 100% return on my investment, which is roughly a 15% CAGR. If it takes longer than 5 years to recover, as ASML did from 2000 to 2012, my annual returns would be much lower, so I tend to avoid longer-recovery stocks unless I can buy them very cheap.

I'll write more about super-cycles later in the article, but for now, I'm going to set that long post-2000 recovery off to the side and shorten our time frame to 2006 so we can get a more recent read on ASML stock behavior during downcycles.

ASML's more recent history shows about a -65% drawdown in 2008/9 with a 3-year recovery time. This, of course, occurred during a very bad recession. Then, we also have a -55% decline in 2021/2 that did not include a recession. The recovery time from that decline was about 2 years. Currently, the stock price is down about -37% off its recent highs from this summer.

I think, all other things roughly equal, we can use these previous declines as a guide for what to expect in the future. Going into 2008, ASML stock was fairly valued and fell about -65% off its highs during a recession. In 2022, ASML was richly valued going into a non-recessionary environment and fell about -55% off its highs. After 2000, ASML was richly valued going into a recessionary environment, and the stock price fell -90%. While it's obvious that things are always changing over a 30-year period, this historical data is very useful for creating a broad framework in which we can think about ASML stock.

Peak P/E ratios & Super Cycles

While I pointed out earlier in the article that fundamental metrics like earnings were not good metrics for valuing deep cyclical businesses, examining peak P/E ratios that occur before earnings declines can give an indication of what the peak valuation was like going into a downturn. This is useful because I will be measuring drawdowns off peak prices, so it stands to reason that if a stock is overvalued going into a downturn, then it might fall deeper than a previous downturn in which it was not overvalued at the start of the downturn.

What is important for this investing strategy is to identify ways in which this downcycle might be different than historical downcycles. In particular, identifying potential super cyclical peaks is important. We can see that the most recent peak had a peak P/E ratio more than double the 2007 peak P/E. This could lead to a deeper downcycle if we have a similar recession as we had in 2008/9.

One additional way I identify supercycles is to simply look at a very long-term log scale price chart and watch for times of extreme upward movement.

When we do this, we see that the 2000 super cycle certainly stands out as a huge upcycle/bubble. The 2007 peak is identifiable as a peak mostly due to the 2008/9 drawdown rather than a huge runup in price ahead of time. In fact, the 2007 peak is more in line with the 2018 peak we saw for ASML. The two recent peaks in 2022 and 2024 look bigger than the 2007 and 2018 peaks, but not as big as the 2000 bubble. So, we probably have experienced a recent boom or super-cycle, but it didn't get as extreme as the one in the late 1990s.

In addition to the recent super cycle, we also need to consider whether the semiconductor industry has an underlying secular growth demand from the increasing use of technology in the modern world, including, of course AI, and the Internet of Things. I think there is plenty of evidence that even though ASML is a cyclical business, it is also in a secular growth industry. This can create a sort of counter-balance to the current downcycle even if it's coming off a super cyclical peak. My take is that the secular growth trend might cancel out some of the super-cycle risk, so the most likely outcome of ASML's current downcycle is somewhere in the middle, or, a "normal" downcycle that is significant, but likely not the worst we have seen historically.

Determining What Prices To Buy

Consistently investing and profiting over the medium term in deep cyclical businesses is difficult. It requires a unique investing approach. You will notice there are many details about ASML's business I have omitted in this article. This is not by accident. There are two important reasons I've done so. The first is that those details are not important. What is important is this is a business with a history of deep earnings cyclicality. The second reason is that often the more we get into the details of a business, the more false sense of security and confidence we have. We can convince ourselves that we know things about the future we do not or cannot know. As evidence of this, there have been 40 "Buy" or "Strong Buy" articles written on ASML this year in Seeking Alpha. Most of them will contain some sort of traditional analysis or deep company or industry insight. Those bullish positions are all underwater now on an absolute basis, and even worse on a relative basis compared to the S&P 500. Conversely, there have been 2 "Sell" articles published during the same time period. This isn't to say industry knowledge isn't important. It is. But it is dwarfed by the importance of understanding cyclicality, and not getting caught up in "stories" and media hype.

One of the things I love about deeply cyclical stocks is that they create situations where precision is unnecessary in order to get great returns. An investor doesn't have to know exactly how far a stock price will fall or exactly how long it will take for it to recover its old highs because even if an investor buys after a -50% drawdown, and it takes the stock 5 full years to recover to a price the market was willing to pay in the past, the returns will still be excellent. I've written dozens of articles about this over the years. The last two successful ones in the semiconductor industry were Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) and Micron (MU) in the fall of 2022, and they both went on to double in less than 2 years. Studying history and ignoring hype are important skills to have when investing in the stocks of cyclical businesses.

Because even during a time when there was no recession, in 2022, ASML has demonstrated it can fall more than -50% off its high, there is never a time I would consider buying this stock when the stock price wasn't at least -50% off its high. However, because there are secular growth trends that remain for their business, I would be willing to buy at that point if the decline gets that deep. So, my first buy price for ASML is at $555.05 per share, about -50% off its recent peak.

But because I know from studying history that ASML has fallen much deeper than that during a recession, I would also 1) be prepared mentally for the possibility that if I buy at $555 the stock could very easily fall much deeper in a recession, and 2) be willing to add to my position if the stock gets down to -65% off its highs.

Conclusion

It is standard practice for stock analysts to put out "price targets", where the goal is to try to predict where a stock price will be in 12 to 18 months. This is not how I approach investing. (Mostly because I don't think fundamentals are good at predicting short-term stock price movements, even if they are good at predicting longer-term stock prices.) My approach is to know the level of returns I would like to achieve in my portfolio over the long term, and to use that to determine the level of returns I think I can get from my stock investments. Once I know that, I can determine the prices for stocks at which my returns goals have a high probability of being met over the medium term of 3-5 years. These are my "Buy Prices". Because I monitor about 300-400 stocks that are high enough quality for me to buy if they hit the right price, I don't need for every stock to hit my buy price. I just need enough of them to do so. This means I don't need to chase stocks, and I can be very selective when it comes to quality and price. If ASML hits my buy price, I'll buy the stock, just like I did with MU and AMD in 2022. And if it doesn't. I will wait for some other opportunity.

data.title: TSLL: Get Out Ahead Of Tesla's Q3 Report, Bearish Post-Earnings Trends
data.description:
Tesla (TSLA) shareholders have endured high volatility in the past handful of quarters. After bottoming at $102 in early 2023, Elon Musk’s darling stock surged to just shy of $300 in the summer of last year. TSLA peaked there, however, and a series of lower highs has ensued in what appears to be a broad consolidation period as the S&P 500 has trended decidedly higher.

Ahead of the Magnificent Seven member’s Q3 report due out on Wednesday, I have a sell rating on the Direxion Daily TSLA Bull 2X Shares ETF (NASDAQ:TSLL). This is technically a downgrade from a hold rating that I made when I last reviewed the fund in November of 2023. Of course, with a leveraged ETF, the typical holding period is very short.

Since Q4 last year, EV prices have soured. The mid-October read of the Manheim Used Vehicle Value Index revealed that EV prices on the secondary market are down by more than 12%, a drop that’s larger than all other used vehicle categories. It’s a headwind for the $705 billion market cap company, and I see downside risks post its Q3 profit report.

EV Prices Down Hard YoY

Investors should recognize the risks associated with owning a leveraged ETF like TSLL. I always like to remind investors, that long-term gains can be high if a protracted trend takes place, but volatility generally hurts such ETFs. Thus, a fund like TSLL should only be used as a short-term trading vehicle, not as a long-term investment, and hence, this article is a near-term tactical outlook on TSLA. For a deeper understanding of the risks tied to leveraged ETFs, you can refer to authoritative bodies such as the SEC. More information can be found in the updated Investor Bulletin titled: Updated Investor Bulletin: Leveraged and Inverse ETFs.

According to the issuer, TSLL seeks daily investment results, before fees and expenses, that are 150% of the performance of the common shares of Tesla. Direxion highlights a major risk with TSLL: Unlike traditional ETFs, or even other leveraged and/or inverse ETFs, these leveraged and/or inverse single-stock ETFs track the price of a single stock rather than an index, eliminating the benefits of diversification. Leveraged and inverse ETFs pursue daily leveraged investment objectives, which means they are riskier than alternatives that do not use leverage.

TSLL is a moderate-sized ETF with $2.0 billion in assets under management as of October 18, 2024. It carries a high 0.96% annual expense ratio and pays a high 6.7% forward dividend yield. Share-price momentum has been lukewarm in the past few months, which is not surprising given TSLA’s up-and-down price action. TSLL is a risky product, evidenced by a poor F rating, according to Seeking Alpha’s quantitative ETF grading system. Finally, liquidity metrics are sound given the fund’s high average daily trading volume of close to 50 million shares.

For a quick take on Tesla’s valuation and earnings outlook, the company trades with a high 96 forward non-GAAP price-to-earnings multiple while its price-to-sales ratio is actually much lower than its 5-year average – now at 7.1 on a forward basis versus a long-term average of 9.5.

Analysts expect $0.60 of normalized EPS to be reported on Wednesday afternoon, which would be about a 10% drop from $0.66 of operating EPS earned in the same period a year ago. Tesla has missed on earnings in each of the past four quarters, so I would not be surprised to see a soft bottom-line number.

Tesla Valuation Metrics: High, But Below Long-Term Averages

Q4 Deliveries Seen Near 546k

Whenever we are discussing short-term, earnings-related investment ideas, it’s helpful to analyze the options market and other historical trends. According to data from Option Research & Technology Services (ORATS), there’s a 6.2% earnings-related stock price swing implied from the current straddle pricing. That’s about a percentage point below the typical implied move of 7.3%.

What should frighten TSLA bulls is that the stock has traded lower post-earnings in five of the past six events, with each of those declines being more than 9%. That’s a bearish trend heading into the Q3 report.

Tesla's Implied Earnings Move is More than 6%, Weak Reaction History

It's not all bearish for the world’s most valuable EV automaker, however. October through February has historically been a strong stretch for TSLA shares, though the past two Octobers have been weak.

Thus, I assert that going short TSLL is a near-term strategy, while going long TSLA once it hits support could be a viable strategy.

Tesla Seasonality: Bullish Q4 Trends

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show a confirmed Q3 2024 earnings date of Wednesday, October 23 AMC with a conference call immediately after the numbers hit the tape. No other volatility catalysts are seen on the calendar.

Tesla's Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With TSLA still priced richly compared to its industry, but somewhat less lofty on valuation versus its own history, the technical situation is mixed. Notice in the chart below that TSLA has a clear downtrend resistance line that currently comes into play around $260. With a flat to slightly negatively sloped long-term 200-day moving average, the bears have an advantage today. Support potentially comes into play at both the 200dma just above $200 and in the mid-$100s.

Based on TSLA’s options pricing, a drop post-earnings to $200 may present a buying opportunity. So, the play would be to short or sell TSLL, then exit that trade if we see a 10% drop in TSLA shares.

Bigger picture, with neutral RSI momentum trends and a high amount of volume by price, I would not be surprised to see volatile price action with no clear trend over the coming weeks, making selling a leveraged long TSLA ETF a solid idea.

TSLA: Ongoing Consolidation, $260 Resistance, Support at the 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a sell rating on TSLL. Going short this leveraged long TSLA ETF would be a winning trade if the trend of poor earnings reactions persists and if TSLA stock continues to trade without a clear uptrend.

1) The Lowdown on Leveraged and Inverse Exchange-Traded Products (FINRA)

2) Leveraged and Inverse ETFs: Specialized Products with Extra Risks for Buy-and-Hold Investors (SEC)

3) FINRA's Reminder on sales practices for Leveraged and Inverse ETFs (FINRA)

data.title: QQQ: An Aggressive Bet On AI Adoption
data.description:
Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (NASDAQ:QQQ) is a tech-focused ETF with considerable outperformance potential in the coming years as artificial intelligence products, both in the hardware and software markets will become available to a broader audience. The ETF further allows investors to build concentrated exposure to Mag 7 stocks, many of which, including NVIDIA (NVDA), have outperformed investor expectations in the last several years. The ETF is obviously only suitable for investors that have a favorable outlook on the technology industry and believe that adoption of artificial intelligence products is going to rise. Despite the near lack of diversification, I believe the ETF could help investors build a broad position in leading AI companies and benefit from growth in this specific market going forward.

Portfolio composition, long-term returns, and AI potential

The Invesco QQQ ETF tracks the Nasdaq 100 Index, which therefore means the ETF is chiefly investing in technology companies. The ETF manages approximately $295B in net assets, and the fund has massively benefited from the tech rally in the last several years. With Invesco QQQ ETF being focused on technology investments, and Mag 7 stocks especially, the ETF has managed to book some impressive returns in the last ten years.

In the last decade, the Invesco QQQ ETF delivered an average annual rate of return of 18.27%. In the last year, the ETF's NAV and price have surged more than 37% as the tech rally went into a new up-leg, driven by the potential for new AI-driven productivity gains in both the hardware and software market.

The Invesco QQQ ETF was established on March 10, 1999 and has, relative to its recent performance numbers, weaker life-to-returns. Since its inception, the Invesco QQQ ETF returned only about half the return it achieved in the last decade, 9.93% annually on average, which is due to the inclusion of the dot-com bust period in the early 2000s that led to a lost decade in tech.

The Invesco QQQ ETF has aggressive allocations to the largest tech names in the industry which is what one would expect from a Nasdaq 100-oriented ETF: Apple (AAPL) has the largest weighting in QQQ with a portfolio representation of 8.9%, followed by chip design company Nvidia with an 8.5% portfolio share and Microsoft (MSFT) which represents 7.8% of the fund’s investments.

Other Mag 7 stocks are also included (see the split below). Readers should note that Alphabet (GOOG), which I consider one of the most egregiously mispriced tech companies -- The Valuation Makes No Sense -- represented 4.7% of total portfolio investments, but its total position was split over different classes of Google's shares.

The main driver of the QQQ, in my opinion, will be the degree to which artificial intelligence products are going to get adopted by corporations and consumers in the future. Stock market indices just reached new highs, which are almost exclusively attributable to the boom in AI spending that has captured the fantasy not only of tech CEOs, but the average investor as well. This new AI industrial revolution has already had a material impact on the valuations of the Mag 7 stocks in the last three years.

The best-performing stock was Nvidia with a return of more than 519% during this time as the company is the ultimate beneficiary of AI-related chip growth. Nvidia benefits enormously from red-hot demand for AI GPUs and is set to launch its Blackwell GPU, which promises a massive leap in inference capabilities. Nvidia stated that the Blackwell GPU was already sold out for the next twelve months, indicating that the spending boom in AI GPUs is durable. The lack of an abundant supply of AI accelerators, at a time when demand is soaring, is why Nvidia has massive revenue outperformance potential in the next 24 months. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM) just reported blow-out Q3'24 earnings, attesting to the same massive demand for AI chips: The Ultimate AI Weather Vane.

AMD’s CEO Lisa Su, to state yet another example of the upswing in the sector, recently said that it sees the Data Center GPU market grow to $500B in revenue by the end of FY 2028 which implies a 10X factor increase compared to FY 2023. AMD is set to be a major beneficiary of the AI GPU spending ramp as well, and the company is now my second-largest position in my portfolio for this very reason.

According to Precedence Research, the market for artificial intelligence (both hardware and software) is set to expand rapidly in the next decade. The market for AI is set to expand to 3.7T by the end of FY 2034, leading to an average annual growth rate of ~20%. This growth is going to be captured chiefly, in my opinion, by Mag 7 companies… many of which have dominant positions in digital advertising, autonomous and electric vehicles, robots, AI chips and consumer technology.

QQQ's valuation

The Invesco QQQ ETF has seen rapid growth in its net asset value in the last ten years and widely outperformed the S&P 500 as investors jumped on the opportunity to invest in large-cap tech companies that benefit from AI-related growth. In the last ten years, the Invesco QQQ ETF has delivered more than twice the returns than an equal-sized investment in an S&P 500 ETF returned.

Risks with QQQ

The biggest upshot of the Invesco QQQ ETF is that the fund is heavily concentrated in Mag 7 companies. In other words, if Mag 7 stocks continue to do well, the QQQ is set to see very strong net asset value growth. On the other hand, since the QQQ is so heavily focused on the technology industry, any underperformance of the sector could lead to a drastic drop in the ETF’s net asset value, exposing investors to steep valuation draw-downs.

Final thoughts

The Invesco QQQ ETF has put up some impressive performance numbers lately, and I don’t believe we are anywhere near the end of the current AI-related spending boom... this is the take-away from Nvidia's recent comments as well as TSMC's third-quarter earnings scorecard. Since the AI theme benefits no other sector more than the technology industry, I believe the Invesco QQQ ETF is worthy of a portfolio over-weighting and most certainly an ETF investors would want to buy on dips, in my opinion. The Mag 7-heavy profile of the Invesco QQQ ETF paired with a low expense ratio of 0.2% and historical outperformance makes QQQ a top bet for growth investors, especially those that have high expectations with regard to AI adoption going forward.

data.title: ClearBridge International Growth ACWI Ex-US Strategy Q3 2024 Commentary
data.description:
By Elisa Mazen, Michael Testorf, CFA & Pawel Wroblewski, CFA

Japanese Reforms Start to Yield Results

Market Overview

Broadening participation sent international equities higher in the third quarter, with value and cyclical shares leading the way as financial conditions eased across most developed markets. The benchmark MSCI All Country World Ex-U.S. Index advanced 8.06%, the MSCI ACWI Ex-U.S. Small Cap Index jumped 9.03%, while the MSCI Emerging Markets Index added 8.72%.

From a regional standpoint, Asia Ex Japan, North America and emerging markets outperformed the index. The United Kingdom performed in line with the index while Europe Ex U.K. and Japan underperformed in a period that saw currency volatility as a rising yen forced carry trade investors who were short the currency to cover their losses. Chinese equities surged to end the quarter (up 23.49%) following the announcement of aggressive stimulus from Beijing, lifting the performance of broader emerging markets.

Sharp reversals were the order of the day in the third quarter, with some of the worst-performing markets such as Hong Kong staging a sharp turnaround after the announcement of large stimulus packages from Chinese officials. Nearly a year's worth of trading took place in the last day of the quarter. This led China-focused segments, like materials, to make significant moves higher. Japan's choice of a new Liberal Democratic Party head to be prime minister also surprised the market, raising fears that he might not follow the corporate policies of the previous administration - something that has since been put to rest - and led to money going back into China from Japan from shorter-term players in the market. Year to date, Japan had been one of the better-performing markets, while China had been unquestionably the worst, hence the strong reversion.

Political drama in France continued with a new prime minister floating the idea of additional corporate tax increases to help salvage the expanding budget deficit; this kept French stocks on their back foot. The 50 basis point Fed rate cut rounded things out at the end of the quarter. Now markets are nervously anticipating the upcoming U.S. election and what that could mean for international trade. The U.S. dollar was broadly weaker throughout the quarter against all major currencies.

International growth stocks rose 6.92% for the quarter but underperformed their value counterparts by 234 basis points as a market rotation away from previous winners in health care and technology ensued.
